name: Puerto del Tiscar and refugio del Collado Zamora - circular gravel through the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
name_original: Puerto del Tiscar y refugio del Collado Zamora- circular gravel por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Quesada traverse a mountainous landscape characterized by significant elevation changes and rugged terrain. The region is part of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park, featuring dense forests, deep valleys, and high mountain passes. Cyclists can expect challenging climbs and rewarding descents through varied natural environments. Best touring cycling routes around Quesada The most popular touring cycling route is Puerto de Tíscar Loop from Quesada, a 22.0 miles (35.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 48 minutes to complete. This route features substantial elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puerto del Tiscar and refugio del Collado Zamora - circular gravel through the P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, a difficult 24.2 miles (38.9 km) path. This route offers a challenging gravel experience through a protected natural park. Local touring cyclists also love the Cazorla – Collado Zamora Refuge loop from Cazorla, a 25.7 miles (41.3 km) trail leading through the high mountains, often completed in about 4 hours 15 minutes. Touring cycling around Quesada is defined by mountainous passes, dense forests, and challenging ascents. The network offers options for experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gains. The routes in Quesada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from around 10 reviews. More than 400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Quesada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Quesada?
A: There are over 60 touring cycling routes around Quesada, offering a wide range of options for exploring the mountainous landscape of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park.

Q: What is the typical terrain and difficulty level for touring cycling in Quesada?
A: Touring cycling around Quesada is characterized by mountainous terrain with significant elevation changes, dense forests, deep valleys, and high mountain passes. The majority of routes are classified as &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt; (41 routes), with a good number of &lt;strong&gt;moderate&lt;/strong&gt; options (19 routes), and a few &lt;strong&gt;easy&lt;/strong&gt; routes (1 route) for less experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Quesada?
A: Yes, many routes around Quesada are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996286932/puerto-de-tiscar-loop-from-quesada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Tíscar Loop from Quesada&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 22.0 miles (35.5 km) with substantial elevation gain. Another challenging circular gravel route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995549946/puerto-del-tiscar-and-refugio-del-collado-zamora-circular-gravel-through-the-p-n-de-las-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Tiscar and refugio del Collado Zamora&lt;/a&gt;, offering a demanding experience through the natural park.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: The routes traverse the stunning Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park, offering views of dense forests, deep valleys, and high mountain passes. You can also discover natural monuments such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459236" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Coloraos Badlands&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3983035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Guadalquivir River&lt;/a&gt;, and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/183641" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Gilillo&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features traditional Spanish villages and citrus groves.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible by touring bike in Quesada?
A: Yes, you can find the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1062504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Linarejos Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; within reach of some touring cycling routes. It's a notable natural monument in the area, offering a scenic stop during your ride.

Q: Are there any huts or refuges along the touring cycling routes for rest stops?
A: Yes, the area around Quesada features several huts and refuges that can serve as rest stops or overnight accommodations. Notable options include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3229805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado Zamora Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3980301" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Hornico Hostel and Nature Center&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3394852" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refugio de la Cañada de los Mojones&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Quesada?
A: The touring cycling routes in Quesada are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 400 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the varied and challenging terrain, often praising the significant elevation gains and the beauty of the natural park.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Quesada?
A: Ciudad Quesada, Spain, benefits from a sunny climate year-round, making it an ideal location for cycling enthusiasts. While the mountainous routes can be challenging, the pleasant climate generally allows for touring cycling throughout most of the year. Always check local weather conditions before heading out, especially for higher elevations.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes suitable for families or beginners?
A: While many routes around Quesada are challenging, there is at least one easy touring cycling route available. For families or beginners, it's advisable to look for routes with minimal elevation gain and shorter distances. The region's diverse landscapes also include flatter coastal and rural paths outside the immediate mountainous area, which might be more suitable for a relaxed family ride.

Q: Is parking available near the trailheads for touring cycling routes?
A: Given the popularity of outdoor activities in the region, parking is generally available near trailheads and key access points to the natural park. However, specific parking availability can vary by route and location, especially in more remote areas. It's recommended to check route details or local information for specific parking facilities.

Q: What kind of elevation gain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Quesada?
A: Touring cycling routes in Quesada are known for significant elevation gains due to the mountainous landscape. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996286932/puerto-de-tiscar-loop-from-quesada" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Tíscar Loop from Quesada&lt;/a&gt; involves over 950 meters of ascent, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995549946/puerto-del-tiscar-and-refugio-del-collado-zamora-circular-gravel-through-the-p-n-de-las-sierras-de-cazorla-segura-y-las-villas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Tiscar and refugio del Collado Zamora&lt;/a&gt; route features over 1100 meters of climbing. Cyclists should be prepared for challenging ascents.

Q: Are there any dog-friendly touring cycling routes in Quesada?
A: While the guide data doesn't specifically highlight dog-friendly routes, many natural parks and rural areas in Spain allow dogs on trails, often requiring them to be on a leash. It's always best to check the specific regulations of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park or individual route details before bringing your dog.
tips:
This is one of the many refuges located on the GR-247 that runs through the entire mountains of Cazorla, Segura and Las Villas. 
It is in very good condition thanks to the care that all of us who pass through it take and clean it and leave it in good condition for use for the following temporary residents.

Following the GR 247 path you will arrive at the port of Tíscar. This port separates the rugged landscapes full of pine forests from a more humanized environment.

As a curiosity, Tíscar means "pass between mountains" in the Berber language.
